The temporomandibular joint is located where the mandible articulates with the skull, directly in front of the auditory canal. Numerous muscles and ligaments guide and control the temporomandibular joint, but at the most basic level, the mandible has 2 possible movements: rotation and translation. When the mouth initially opens, the mandible rotates and then slides forward at a certain point to allow further opening, a process known as translation. Elevators should be applied perpendicular to the long axis of the tooth, with both parts of the elevator placed on hard surfaces. The inner aspect of the elevator tip should be placed on tooth tissue. The outer aspect of the elevator tip should be placed on the alveolar bone and not on the neighboring tooth. Document [29] (Title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Equipment -- Luxators) Luxators look similar to elevators; however, they are used with different aims. Document [31] (Title: Mandible Dislocation -- Treatment / Management) External Approach: It is performed by placing the patient in a seated position with the provider facing the patient. The provider places the fingers of one hand on the ascending ramus, grasping the mandibular angle. Thumb placement is on the malar eminence of the maxilla or zygomatic bone. The opposite side is positioned using the thumb above the displaced coronoid process, applying posterior pressure. The fingers of the same hand pull traction on the mastoid process of the temporal bone. The simultaneous action of both hands is performed to reduce the mandible. The angle of the mandible is pulled anteriorly while on the opposite side, the coronoid process is pushed posteriorly and directed into the temporal fossa. One side of the mandible will reduce. If the opposite side does not reduce spontaneously immediately following the first reduction, reverse hand positions and reduce the second side. [26]

Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026) Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric problems. Among these, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation in which a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before birth, thereby improving the prospects for a vaginal delivery. Cicatrix of cervix affecting pregnancy refers to a cervical scar or fibrous tissue that can compromise cervical integrity and potentially interfere with the progress of pregnancy and the mechanics of labor. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes the presence of a cervical neoplasm that may pose risks to both maternal health and fetal development, often necessitating careful monitoring and tailored delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) describes abnormal positioning of the fetus, such as breech or transverse lie, which can impede normal labor progression and may require interventions such as external cephalic version, operative delivery, or cesarean section. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which maternal cervical pathology and fetal positioning can impact labor and delivery, underscoring the importance of accurate diagnosis and individualized management to optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

Document [6] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2042) MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY encompasses a range of obstetric factors that influence the course of pregnancy, labor, and birth. Document [13] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2265) Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery comprise a broad spectrum of obstetric scenarios that influence how birth is managed. The category includes routine situations such as a normal pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery, as well as standard delivery procedures that are applied when labor progresses without complication. It also encompasses a variety of multiple‑gestation pregnancies—twin (حمل توأمي), triplet (حمل بثلاثة توائم), quadruplet (حمل بأربعة توائم), and even septuplet (Septuplet pregnancy) pregnancies—each presenting unique challenges for monitoring, timing of delivery, and neonatal care. Fetal malpresentation is another important element within this group; examples include poor fetal positioning (سوء تموضع الجنين) and breech presentation that has been successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, illustrating how obstetric interventions can correct unfavorable fetal positions to facilitate a safer vaginal birth. Together, these conditions highlight the need for individualized assessment and tailored management strategies to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.
Document [14] (Title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894) The category “MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY” includes a range of specific child conditions that can markedly influence the course of childbirth. Among these are breech malpresentation that has been successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, septuplet pregnancy, حمل بأربعة توائم (a pregnancy with quadruplets), حمل توأمي (twin pregnancy), and سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition). These entities represent complex fetal presentations and multiple‑gestation pregnancies, each requiring specialized obstetric management to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.
Document [15] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028) Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381) MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad classification that includes several specific obstetric entities. Among these are Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation, which represents a fetal position abnormality that has been corrected to the optimal head‑first orientation for delivery. Cervical cerclage suture present denotes the placement of a supportive stitch around the cervix to prevent premature dilation in at‑risk pregnancies. Rigid cervix uteri affecting pregnancy describes a condition in which cervical stiffness impedes normal cervical remodeling and can compromise gestational progress. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy refers to cervical neoplasms that may interfere with pregnancy maintenance and the mechanics of labor. Finally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) is another form of abnormal fetal orientation that can impact the course of labor. All of these entities are considered child conditions within the overarching category of MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY, highlighting their collective relevance to the assessment, management, and outcomes of labor and delivery.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2335) MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric scenarios. Among them, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation where a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before delivery, improving the likelihood of a vaginal birth. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes a cervical neoplasm that can complicate gestation and influence the mode and safety of delivery. The category also encompasses حمل بأربعة توائم (a quadruple pregnancy), which presents heightened risks for both mother and fetuses and often requires specialized monitoring and delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) refers to abnormal positioning of the fetus that can impede the progress of labor. MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is related to Normal pregnancy &/or delivery (spontaneous vaginal delivery), indicating that when these conditions are appropriately managed or resolved, a normal, uncomplicated vaginal delivery may still be achievable. Understanding how each of these conditions interacts with labor and delivery is essential for optimizing maternal and fetal outcomes.

Together, these entities represent distinct yet interconnected aspects of uterine functional disorders that can influence the mechanics of labor and delivery.

Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2064) External obstetrical version is classified as a fetal procedure and belongs to a broader category of obstetric interventions. It is performed on the uterus, where the practitioner attempts to reposition the fetus externally. Manual conversion of position is another obstetric technique that also takes place in the uterus, involving manipulation to change fetal orientation. Both procedures share the same anatomical site—the uterus—and are used to correct fetal malpresentation, highlighting their related roles in obstetric care.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2386) Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery comprise a range of obstetric issues that can influence the course and outcome of childbirth. Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation illustrates a scenario in which an abnormal fetal position is corrected before labor, facilitating a safer vaginal delivery. Polyp of cervix affecting pregnancy represents a cervical lesion that may interfere with cervical competence, increase the risk of bleeding, or complicate the progression of labor. Wanrenhouding; foetale hydrops (fetal hydrops) denotes a severe accumulation of fluid in fetal compartments, a condition that can jeopardize fetal well‑being and necessitate specialized delivery planning. حمل بثلاثة توائم (pregnancy with three twins, i.e., triplet pregnancy) introduces multiple‑fetus dynamics that heighten the risk of preterm labor, malpresentation, and maternal strain, requiring careful monitoring and often a tailored delivery approach. سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) refers to any deviation from the optimal fetal orientation for birth, such as breech or transverse lie, which can complicate labor progress and may require operative intervention. Together, these conditions underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and individualized management strategies to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148) MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a classification that includes several specific fetal malposition entities. Among these, Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a scenario in which a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the optimal head‑first (cephalic) orientation, facilitating a safer vaginal delivery. The category also encompasses the term wanverhouding; foetaal, a Dutch expression referring to abnormal fetal positioning, and سوء تموضع الجنين, the Arabic phrase for fetal malposition. Together, these conditions highlight the importance of recognizing and managing atypical fetal presentations to optimize labor outcomes and reduce delivery complications.

Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1497) MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that encompasses several specific maternal and fetal abnormalities that can influence the course of labor and delivery. Within this category are conditions such as Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation, Polyp of cervix affecting pregnancy, Rigid cervix uteri affecting pregnancy, and the Arabic‑language condition سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition). These conditions are directly related to the processes of Normal delivery procedure and Normal pregnancy &/or delivery (& spontaneous vaginal delivery), indicating that they must be considered when planning and managing routine vaginal births. Recognizing and appropriately managing each of these child conditions under the umbrella of MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is essential for achieving optimal maternal and neonatal outcomes during normal labor and delivery.
